messages.content:
I need to write a vba code That will cut the following file 'Due Checks.csv' from this address
C:\Users\HP\Downloads\Due Checks.csv
and paste into this folder
G:\Shared drives\Swan School Site Premises\PREMISES MANAGEMENT\SERVICE PROVIDERS
automatically overwriting any same named file, without any prompts

Here is the VBA code that will cut the file 'Due Checks.csv' from the source address and paste it in the destination folder, automatically overwriting any existing file with the same name without any prompts.

Sub CutAndPasteFile()

'Get the source and destination paths
Dim srcPath As String
Dim dstPath As String

srcPath = "C:\Users\HP\Downloads\Due Checks.csv"
dstPath = "G:\Shared drives\Swan School Site Premises\PREMISES MANAGEMENT\SERVICE PROVIDERS\Due Checks.csv"

'Check if the destination file already exists and delete it if yes
If Dir(dstPath) <> "" Then
    Kill dstPath
End If

'Cut and paste the file
FileCopy srcPath, dstPath
Kill srcPath

End Sub

Note: The code assumes that the source and destination folders already exist. If they do not exist, the code will return an error.
When using Power Query to import data from a csv, there is a box that says "Add this Data to the Data Model". What does this mean and what does it do?

"Add this data to the Data Model" is an option in Power Query when importing data from a CSV file or any other data source. 

If you check this option while importing data, the data will not only be loaded into the worksheet as a table, but it will also be added to the Power Pivot data model, which is basically a database engine that allows for quick and efficient data analysis.

When data is added to the data model, it becomes available for use in other Microsoft Office applications like Excel, Power BI, or even Access. You can do things like building pivot tables and charts based on the data model, create relationships between tables, and analyze large amounts of data with greater precision and speed.

By using the data model, you're essentially creating a centralized data source that can be used across multiple reports and analyses, saving you time and effort in the long run.
Is it possible to use a code to refresh a Power Query Data sheet without opening the sheet and having to press the button Refresh

Yes, it's possible to refresh a Power Query Data sheet using VBA code without having to open the sheet or manually pressing the Refresh button. You can automate this process by writing a macro or VBA code that triggers the refresh process.

Here is an example VBA code that refreshes a Power Query Data sheet:

```
Sub RefreshPowerQueryData()
    Dim ws As Worksheet
    Dim qt As QueryTable
    
    Set ws = Worksheets("Sheet1") 'Change to your worksheet name
    Set qt = ws.QueryTables(1) 'Change to your query table index 
    qt.Refresh BackgroundQuery:=False 'Change to True if you want to refresh in the background
End Sub
```

In this code, we first define the worksheet where the Power Query Data sheet is located and the QueryTable object that we want to refresh. Then, we call the Refresh method of the QueryTable object and pass an optional argument BackgroundQuery to specify if we want to refresh the query in the background or not.

You can call this macro from another VBA procedure or from a button on your Excel sheet to execute the refresh process.
How do I get my query table index     Set qt = ws.QueryTables(1)

You can get the query table index by counting the number of QueryTables on the worksheet that contains your Power Query Data table. 

Here's the VBA code that you can use to get the query table index:

```
Sub GetQueryTableIndex()
    Dim ws As Worksheet
    Dim qt As QueryTable
    Dim i As Integer
    
    Set ws = ActiveSheet 'Change to your worksheet name
    i = 1 'Start with index 1
    
    For Each qt In ws.QueryTables
        Debug.Print "QueryTable " & i & ": " & qt.Name
        i = i + 1
    Next qt
End Sub
```

In this code, we first define the worksheet where the Power Query Data table is located. Then, we loop through all the QueryTables on that worksheet and use the Debug.Print statement to output the name and index of each QueryTable to the Immediate window.

When you run this macro, you can see the index of each QueryTable printed in the Immediate window. You can then use the index number to update the following line in your VBA code: `Set qt = ws.QueryTables(1)` by replacing `1` with the appropriate index number.

Please note that if you don't have any QueryTables on your worksheet, then the code will throw an error.

I am getting an error Memeber of Data table not found on the line; Set qt = ws.Queries(1) 'Change to your query table index     in:   Sub RefreshPowerQueryData()

Dim ws As Worksheet
Dim qt As QueryTable

Set ws = ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Due Checks") 'Change to your worksheet name
'Set qt = ws.QueryTables(1) 'Change to your query table index
Set qt = ws.Queries(1) 'Change to your query table index
qt.Refresh BackgroundQuery:=False 'Change to True if you want to refresh in the background

End Sub

The error message "Member of Data table not found" suggests that the query table you're trying to reference does not exist in the workbook. 

The issue is most likely due to the fact that you're trying to use the `ws.Queries()` method instead of the `ws.QueryTables()` method. 

The `ws.Queries()` method returns a collection of Power Query queries, which are different from QueryTables. 

To fix the issue, replace `ws.Queries(1)` with `ws.QueryTables(1)` to reference the first QueryTable on the "Due Checks" worksheet:

Dim ws As Worksheet
Dim qt As QueryTable

Set ws = ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Due Checks") 'Change to your worksheet name
Set qt = ws.QueryTables(1) 'Change to your query table index
qt.Refresh BackgroundQuery:=False 'Change to True if you want to refresh in the background

End Sub

This updated code should correctly reference the first QueryTable on the "Due Checks" worksheet and allow you to refresh the query using VBA.
how do i then find my Query Table Index

Here's how you can find the QueryTable index:

1. Open the worksheet that contains your Power Query table.
2. Go to the "Data" tab in the Excel ribbon, and click on the "Queries & Connections" button in the "Get & Transform Data" section.
3. This will open the "Workbook Queries" window that displays all the queries and tables in your workbook. 
4. Find the query name for the Power Query table or data you want to refresh.
5. Click on the query name to select it.
6. The index of the QueryTable is displayed in the "Properties" pane on the right-hand side of the window under the "Connection Properties" section under the "Definition" tab.

Alternatively, you can find the QueryTable index using VBA by running the following code:

```
Sub GetQueryTableIndex()
    Dim ws As Worksheet
    Dim qt As QueryTable
    Dim i As Integer
    
    Set ws = ActiveSheet 'Change to your worksheet name
    i = 1 'Start with index 1
    
    For Each qt In ws.QueryTables
        Debug.Print "QueryTable " & i & ": " & qt.Name
        i = i + 1
    Next qt
End Sub
```

This code loops through each QueryTable on the active worksheet and outputs the name and index of each QueryTable to the Immediate window. When you run this macro, you can find the index of the QueryTable you want to reference and use the following code to refresh it:

```
Set qt = ws.QueryTables(query_table_index) 'Replace query_table_index with the index of the query table you want to refresh.
qt.Refresh BackgroundQuery:=False
``` 

Remember to replace "ws" with the worksheet object name that contains your Power Query table. The above code will refresh the Power Query table corresponding to the specified QueryTable index.

Application defined or Object defined error in: Sub RefreshPowerQueryData()

Dim ws As Worksheet
Dim qt As QueryTable

Set ws = ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Due Checks") 'Change to your worksheet name
Set qt = QueryTables(Table_Due_Checks) 'Change to your query table index
qt.Refresh BackgroundQuery:=False 'Change to True if you want to refresh in the background

End Sub

The error "Application defined or Object defined error" usually occurs when there is an issue with the object or syntax used in the code.

In your code, the issue is with the syntax used to reference the QueryTable object. `QueryTables` is not a valid object or property, so it's throwing an error.

To reference the QueryTable object, you should use the `Worksheets("Due Checks").QueryTables("Table_Due_Checks")` syntax. The `"Table_Due_Checks"` should be replaced with the actual name of the query table.

Here's the updated code with the correct syntax:

```
Sub RefreshPowerQueryData()
    Dim ws As Worksheet
    Dim qt As QueryTable
    
    Set ws = ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Due Checks") 'Change to your worksheet name
    Set qt = ws.QueryTables("Table_Due_Checks") 'Change to your query table name
    qt.Refresh BackgroundQuery:=False 'Change to True if you want to refresh in the background
End Sub
```

This updated code should correctly reference the `QueryTable` object and allow you to refresh the query using VBA.
what is a good conditional formatting code to find duplicates in column K and highlight column J

Here's an example VBA code that you can use to highlight duplicates in Column K and corresponding cells in Column J:

```
Sub HighlightDuplicates()
    Dim lastRow As Long
    Dim i As Long
    Dim dict As Object
    Dim key As Variant
    Dim rng As Range
    
    Set dict = CreateObject("Scripting.Dictionary")
    lastRow = Range("K" & Rows.Count).End(xlUp).Row
    
    'Loop through each cell in Column K and add to dictionary if not already present
    For i = 1 To lastRow
        If Not dict.exists(Range("K" & i).Value) Then
            dict.Add Range("K" & i).Value, Range("J" & i)
        Else 'Highlight the corresponding cells in Column J if duplicate found
            Set rng = Range("J" & i)
            rng.Interior.ColorIndex = 6 'Change color to your preferred highlight color
            dict(Range("K" & i).Value).Interior.ColorIndex = 6 'Change color to your preferred highlight color
        End If
    Next i
End Sub
```

This code uses a dictionary to store the unique values in Column K. If a duplicate is found, it highlights the corresponding cells in Column J using a color of your choice. 

To change the highlight color, update the line `rng.Interior.ColorIndex = 6` and `dict(Range("K" & i).Value).Interior.ColorIndex = 6` according to your preferred color index.

You can run this macro by pressing Alt + F8 to open the Macro dialog box and selecting the HighlightDuplicates macro from the list of macros.
Thanks, but I need a Conditional Format formula to highlight duplicates in a column

No worries! Here's a conditional formatting formula that highlights duplicates in Column K, and the corresponding cells in Column J:

1. Select the range of cells in both Column J and K that you want to apply the conditional formatting to.
2. Then, go to the "Home" tab in the Excel ribbon and click on "Conditional Formatting" --> "New Rule".
3. In the "New Formatting Rule" dialog box, select "Use a formula to determine which cells to format".
4. In the "Format values where this formula is true" box, enter the following formula:

`=COUNTIF($K:$K,$K1)>1`

This formula counts the number of times the value in Column K appears in the entire Column K and returns `TRUE` if it is greater than 1, which means it has duplicates in the column. The `$` sign locks the column reference while allowing the row reference to change as the formula is applied to each row.
5. Click the "Format" button to choose the highlight color or other formatting options that you prefer for the duplicate values.
6. Click "OK" to apply the conditional formatting rule.

This should highlight the duplicate values in Column K and the corresponding cells in Column J with your chosen formatting.
what does the &gt in the formula represent

`>` is an HTML entity that represents the greater-than sign (>). In other words, it's a way of representing the greater-than symbol in HTML code.

In the specific context you're referring to, it's likely that you encountered this code in a web page or online forum where the code was displayed as HTML. The ">" entity in the formula simply represents the greater-than symbol (>) in the actual formula.

When using the formula in your Excel worksheet, you should replace the ">" entity with the actual greater-than symbol (>).

For example, the formula "`=IF(A1>>B1,"TRUE","FALSE")`" should be written as "`=IF(A1>B1,"TRUE","FALSE")`" in your Excel worksheet.
